sourceSections: The Enduring Value of Software Fundamentals in the AI Age
Fostering Shared Understanding with AI for Design Clarity
Cultivating a Ubiquitous Language for Precision
Driving Quality Through Feedback Loops and Test-Driven Development
Strategic Module Design for AI Collaboration and Cognitive Load Management

sections.lead: Software fundamentals are crucial in the AI age, as bad code, increasingly costly, prevents AI from realizing its full potential.

bullets.label: Critique Specs
bullets.text: The speaker criticizes the "specs-to-code" movement, where AI generates code from specifications, for producing unmanageable code due to neglecting direct code engagement.

bullets.label: Define Bad Code
bullets.text: Drawing from "A Philosophy of Software Design" and "The Pragmatic Programmer," bad code is defined as complex, hard to change, and prone to "software entropy" if system design is overlooked.

bullets.label: Cost of Bad Code
bullets.text: The assertion that "code is cheap" is dismissed, as bad code is now incredibly expensive, hindering AI's potential within a codebase.

bullets.label: AI Performance
bullets.text: Good codebases are essential for AI to perform well, reinforcing the importance of foundational software engineering principles.

sections.lead: AI often generates unwanted outputs due to a lack of shared design concepts, which the "Grill Me" skill resolves through adversarial questioning.

bullets.label: AI Misalignment
bullets.text: A common failure occurs when the AI generates something unexpected or unwanted, stemming from a lack of a "design concept" shared between the human and AI.

bullets.label: Brooks' Analogy
bullets.text: This challenge is akin to human-to-human collaboration difficulties described in Frederick P. Brooks' "The Design of Design."

bullets.label: Grill Me Skill
bullets.text: The speaker developed the "Grill Me" skill, which prompts the AI to relentlessly interview the user about every design aspect until a mutual understanding is established.

bullets.label: Refine Requirements
bullets.text: This adversarial questioning process, often involving 40-100 questions, helps refine requirements and leads to more aligned and useful outputs.

bullets.label: Improved Outputs
bullets.text: Outputs include detailed product requirements documents or direct issues, surpassing the eagerness of default AI planning modes.

sections.lead: AI verbosity and miscommunication are addressed by a "ubiquitous language" skill, which scans codebases to foster shared terminology and improve accuracy.

bullets.label: AI Verbosity
bullets.text: Another common failure involves AI being overly verbose or communicating at cross-purposes, mirroring the language gaps often seen between human developers and domain experts.

bullets.label: DDD Inspiration
bullets.text: Inspired by Domain-Driven Design (DDD) and its concept of a "ubiquitous language," the speaker created a skill to address this.

bullets.label: Terminology Compilation
bullets.text: The "ubiquitous language" skill scans a codebase to extract and compile terminology, generating a shared markdown file.

bullets.label: Communication Clarity
bullets.text: This common vocabulary, actively used by both human and AI, enables more concise AI communication and significantly improves planning accuracy.

bullets.label: Implementation Alignment
bullets.text: The skill ensures that the implementation aligns more closely with the intended design, proving to be an exceptionally powerful tool.

sections.lead: Robust feedback loops and TDD are crucial for AI-generated code, guiding small steps to prevent errors and ensure quality.

bullets.label: Code Not Working
bullets.text: Even when AI understands the task and generates code, it might not work, highlighting the importance of robust feedback loops.

bullets.label: Feedback Examples
bullets.text: These feedback loops include static types, browser access for front-end development, and automated tests.

bullets.label: LLM Issue
bullets.text: A critical issue is that LLMs often "outrun their headlights," producing large amounts of code before checking for errors.

bullets.label: Speed Limit Principle
bullets.text: The speaker emphasizes that "the rate of feedback is your speed limit," meaning AI should be guided to take small, deliberate steps.

bullets.label: TDD Solution
bullets.text: The recommended solution is to employ Test-Driven Development (TDD), which forces the AI to create tests first, make them pass, and then refactor.

bullets.label: Quality Improvement
bullets.text: This disciplined approach ensures incremental progress and higher code quality, underpinned by the insight that "good codebases are easy to test."

sections.lead: Strategic module design, prioritizing "deep modules" over shallow ones, reduces human cognitive load and improves AI collaboration through clear interfaces.

bullets.label: Shallow Modules
bullets.text: AI frequently generates codebases filled with "shallow modules"—many small units with complex interfaces—making them hard for both humans and AI to understand and navigate.

bullets.label: Deep Modules Goal
bullets.text: Drawing from John Ousterhout, the recommendation is to aim for "deep modules" with extensive functionality hidden behind simple interfaces, effectively managing complexity.

bullets.label: Architecture Skill
bullets.text: A skill called "Improve codebase architecture" helps refactor shallow modules into deep ones, creating clear, testable boundaries.

bullets.label: Delegation Strategy
bullets.text: This architectural approach allows developers to "design the interface and delegate the implementation" to the AI, treating modules as "gray boxes."

bullets.label: Cognitive Load
bullets.text: This significantly reduces cognitive load for human developers by allowing them to focus on strategic design and external verification, rather than reviewing every line of AI-generated code, especially for less critical components.

sections.lead: Code is fundamentally important; humans must embrace a strategic design role, guiding AI as a tactical programmer with strong foundational principles.

bullets.label: Code Importance
bullets.text: The overarching message is that code is not cheap; it is fundamentally important.

bullets.label: AI's Tactical Role
bullets.text: In the AI age, AI functions as a tactical, on-the-ground programmer.

bullets.label: Human's Strategic Role
bullets.text: Human developers must ascend to a strategic role, investing daily in the system's design.

bullets.label: Foundational Principles
bullets.text: This strategic oversight requires a strong foundation in traditional software engineering principles, empowering developers to effectively harness AI and make a significant impact.

bullets.label: Available Resources
bullets.text: Resources for the discussed skills and further training are available via the macpocockskills GitHub repo and aihero.dev.
